summary: Jeff Atwood argues that API and developer tool usability should be approached with the same rigor as end-user usability testing. Drawing on Microsoft researcher Steven Clarke's usability studies, he demonstrates that developers face the same cognitive challenges as regular users when learning new systems. The post highlights how observation-based usability testing revealed problems with .NET APIs that weren't obvious to their creators. Atwood emphasizes that while good code samples are crucial for developer experience, they shouldn't be used to paper over fundamentally flawed API designs. The core message is that when developers struggle with your API, the problem likely lies in your design, not in their competence.
key_points: Developer usability testing uses the same methods as end-user testing: personas, use cases, tasks, and observation
Code samples are often the first thing developers look for and set the tone for their entire experience with a new technology
Good documentation cannot compensate for APIs that expose abstractions at the wrong level
When developers can't find what they're looking for in docs, it may be because the expected abstraction simply doesn't exist in the API
If you're constantly answering questions about code you've written, the problem is likely your design, not your colleagues
money_quotes: When it comes to usability, there's no substitute for observation. It's absolute bedrock.
The reason the developers weren't successful when they were searching through the help was because what they were looking for simply didn't exist.
If you're answering a lot of questions from other developers about something you've written, it's probably not because your co-workers suck.
The API they were working with exposed a set of abstractions that were at the wrong level for these particular developers.
You have to be careful that you're not papering over bad API design decisions with good code samples.
key_insight: APIs deserve the same usability testing rigor as consumer products, and frequent developer questions about your code signal design problems, not user incompetence.
